description:

You just [revealed the real, less glamorous truth].

You think you’re [celebrating/winning]? 

You just [what they actually did instead, emotionally charged].

The moment [event happens]:

- You can’t [important loss #2]
- You can’t [important loss #3]
- You can’t [important loss #4]

[Scenario A with specific steps/numbers]

No [external validation 1].

You didn't raise 0M.

You just became an EMPLOYEE at your own company.

After watching 100+ founders take VC money, here's the TRUTH nobody will tell you:

That wire transfer was your RESIGNATION letter.

You think you're celebrating? 

You just sold your FREEDOM for a salary you don't even control.

The moment that 0M hits:
- You can't pay yourself without board approval
- You can't hire your brother without committee votes
- You can't pivot without a 47-slide deck
- You can't sell without liquidation preferences

You're not a CEO. 

You're middle management with equity that's worth NOTHING until VCs get paid first.

Here's the MATH that will make you sick:

Raise 0M at 0M valuation:
- Day 1: You own 75%
- After option pool: 65%
- After Series B: 40%
- After down round: 15%
- After "strategic" CEO: 5%

7 years. 100-hour weeks. 5% ownership.

Your exit at 0M? 
- VCs take first 0M (preferences) 
- You get 5% of 0M = 00k

00k for 7 years = 1k/year That's LESS than a junior developer.

Meanwhile, I watched a founder:
- Bootstrap to M revenue
- Sell for 4x = 2M
- Own 100% = 2M cash
- Time to exit: 24 months

No TechCrunch article. 
No podcast interviews. 
Just 2 MILLION in their account.

Every founder raising money thinks they're building wealth.

They're building wealth. Just not THEIRS.

The best cap table isn't diverse. It's EMPTY. Just your name.

name: Value Multiplier Post
description:
This format works by leading with a counterintuitive lesson, backing it with authority, then revealing the hidden consequence most people miss. The multiplier framework (10x, 100x) makes the value gap concrete and creates urgency around taking action.

Template:
structure:
My biggest lesson on [topic]:

When you spot [a high-quality asset/person/situation], [extreme/counterintuitive action].

[Amplify the action/benefit], make [them/it] feel [unique status].

[Authority Figure] said: 
[Famous relevant quote or framework].

[He/She] was right.

But here's what [he/she] didn't say:

If you [negative action related to the quote], [initial negative consequence].
Then [second-tier negative consequence].
Then [third-tier negative consequence/the domino effect].

One [high-quality option] is [Multiplier, e.g., 10x] better than [a standard option].
And [Multiplier, e.g., 100x] better than [a poor option].

[Action step] accordingly.

The best [people/assets/tools] aren't expensive.
Losing them is.

[Final action/vision statement] and [keep/maintain/grow].
original_post_text:
My biggest lesson on people:

When you spot an outperformer, Pay them 10x.

Overpay them, make them feel special.

Steve Jobs said: 
A-players hire A-players. 
B-players hire C-players.

He was right.

But here's what he didn't say:

If you underpay your A-players, they leave.
Then your B-players take over.
Then your B-players hire C-players.

One A-player is 10x better than a good B-player.
And 100x better than a C.

Pay accordingly.

The best people aren't expensive.
Losing them is.

Inspire them and keep them.

original_post_text:
Meet my buddy Spencer Scott 
He made 0,000 in 11 days.
He QUIT his SaaS startup to pick up garbage.
No code, no investors, no burnout.

Here's the anti-Silicon Valley playbook:

Spencer was scrolling Facebook when his neighbors started raging. 
Their trash company got bought out and was suddenly charging 0 extra fees for bulk pickup.

Instead of complaining like everyone else, Spencer posted:

"If you're all as mad as you say, I'll start a trash company."

150 angry comments exploded on that post.

Spencer realized he had something.

He threw up a Stripe link: "If 200 people commit to 3 months at 3/month, I'll do this."

10 days later: 0,000 raised.
150 customers signed up.
,000 monthly recurring revenue.

Before owning a single truck.

The craziest part? Spencer bought his garbage truck sight unseen for 5K while his wife was giving birth.

"What was I going to do, fly out there and say 'yep, looks like a trash truck'?"

45 days from Facebook post to picking up his first route.

The plot twist nobody saw coming:

Kids now run out of their houses when they see Spencer's truck. 
Neighbors bake him lunch. He's become a local celebrity.

"In software, no one ever gave me a high five. In trash, people bring me peanut butter sandwiches."

Spencer's insight: "It's exactly like SaaS - monthly recurring revenue. Except instead of debugging code, I pick up trash once a week."

The business fundamentals that make VCs cry: 
• One day per week operations 
• 20% profit margins at scale 
• 7x EBITDA multiple when you sell 
• Zero outside investment needed 
• Recession-proof demand

While we're all worried about AI taking our jobs, Spencer found the most automation-proof business in America.

And it's paying better than most tech salaries.

original_post_text:
How to become indispensable at work.

7 underrated career skills everyone should know:

1️⃣ Be teachable
↳ You don’t know everything, and you’re not always right. The best people to work with are the ones who stay curious and open to learning.

2️⃣ Be easy to work with
↳ Talent is great, but if working with you feels like a chore, people will avoid it. Make life easier for others, and opportunities will follow.

3️⃣ Be quick to admit mistakes
↳ No one expects perfection. Own your mistakes, fix them fast, and move on.

4️⃣ Be reliable 
↳ The bar is lower than you think. Follow up, follow through, and show up consistently.

5️⃣ Be generous with credit
↳ Wins are rarely achieved alone. Acknowledge others, and you’ll build goodwill that compounds over time.

6️⃣ Be the one who does more than expected
↳ Get in the habit of going above and beyond. The extra effort is often what sets you apart from the pack.

7️⃣ Be the person who never stops learning
↳ Jobs change. Industries evolve. Those who keep learning and improving never have to worry about staying relevant. 

The best way to future-proof your career? 
Become the person no one wants to lose.

name: The Price of Honesty
description: This format works by sharing a specific moment where choosing integrity over a "quick win" cost you a deal in the short term, but built your reputation in the long term. It positions you as an expert who values results over empty promises.
structure:
I lost [a high-value deal/opportunity] because I told the truth.

The [prospect/client] asked: "[Difficult/Loaded question]?"

The honest answer: "[Realistic but 'slow' answer]". 
The competitive answer: "[Unrealistic but 'fast' answer]".

I chose honesty.

His response: "[Competitor name/type] says they can do it in [half the time/half the cost]."

Me: "[Your professional rebuttal explaining the complexity]."

Him: "I'll take my chances with them."

[Timeframe] later, [he/she] called back.
Their "[unrealistic]" project was still [broken/unfinished] after [Timeframe]. 
They'd spent 0 and had nothing to show for it.

"Can you fix this mess?" [he/she] asked.

We did. In [Timeframe]. Exactly like I predicted.

Here's the brutal truth about [your industry]:

Honesty is expensive in the short term. 
Lies are expensive in the long term.

I could have [common industry shortcut 1] to win deals. 
I could have [common industry shortcut 2]. 
I could have played the [common industry game, e.g., 'fake it till you make it'].

Instead, I chose transparency.

Lost deals in [early stage]. Won trust in [later stage].

The [clients/customers] who chose us because we told the truth became our biggest advocates.

The ones who chose our competitors? 
They all came back eventually.

Your reputation is built on promises kept, not promises made.

Sometimes the most profitable strategy is just being honest about what you can't do.
original_post_text:
I lost a 00K deal because I told the truth.

The prospect asked: "How long will this take?"

The honest answer: "4 months". 
The competitive answer: "6-8 weeks".

I chose honesty.

His response: "Your competitor says they can do it in half the time."

Me: "They're either lying or cutting corners. This is complex work."

Him: "I'll take my chances with them."

Six months later, he called back.
Their "6-week" project was still broken after 5 months. 
They'd spent 50K and had nothing to show for it.

"Can you fix this mess?" he asked.

We did. In 4 months. Exactly like I predicted.

Here's the brutal truth about business:

Honesty is expensive in the short term. 
Lies are expensive in the long term.

I could have promised unrealistic timelines to win deals. 
I could have overstated our capabilities. 
I could have played the Silicon Valley "fake it till you make it" game.

Instead, I chose transparency.

Lost deals in year one. Won trust in year five.

The clients who chose us because we told the truth became our biggest advocates.

The ones who chose our competitors? 
They all came back eventually.

Your reputation is built on promises kept, not promises made.

Sometimes the most profitable strategy is just being honest about what you can't do.

original_post_text:
Stop justifying your boundaries. 

These 12 phrases command instant respect 👇🏼

I used to say yes to everything:

- Last minute requests at 5pm
- Weekend emails "that couldn't wait" 
- "Quick" calls that stole my focus time

All because I didn't know how to say no.

Everything changed when I switched to these 12 power phrases ✨

1) "That won't work for me, but here's what will" 
↳ Redirects the conversation without justification

2) "I'm unavailable after 6pm, I can help tomorrow at 9" 
↳ Sets time limits without room for negotiation

3) "I need to check my schedule before answering" 
↳ Creates thinking space to avoid reactive yeses

4) "My calendar is blocked for deep work until 2pm" 
↳ Establishes professional barriers few will challenge

5) "Which of these priorities should I set aside?" 
↳ Shifts responsibility while showing your full plate

6) "I've set aside 30 minutes for this conversation" 
↳ Time-boxes interactions that tend to expand endlessly

7) "I can help with part A and B, but not part C" 
↳ Offers partial help instead of total rejection

8) "I don't respond to messages on weekends" 
↳ Sets expectations that prevent future disappointment

9) "This needs my full attention - can we schedule it?" 
↳ Transforms demands into planned interactions

10) "I've learned I can't take last-minute requests" 
↳ Frames boundary as growth, not personal choice

11) "Let me think and get back to you by [time]" 
↳ Creates space without tension or commitment

12) "That's outside my zone of wisdom, here's who can help" 
↳ Turns rejection into helpful redirection

Strong boundaries don't require explanation. 
They require the right words at the right moment ✨

Getting emotionally attached to your job.

Companies don't love you back.

That's not an opinion. It's fact.

When you attach to companies:

→ They change direction, you lose focus
→ They cut budgets, you lose stability
→ They pivot strategy, you lose position

Instead, attach to purpose.

Here's how:

- Write down your core values
- List what truly matters to you
- Set your own success metrics

2/ Build Your Foundation

- Create a personal brand
- Own your skill development
- Network outside your company

3/ Play The Long Game

- Document your wins
- Build systems, not just skills
- Focus on market value, not job title

Because when you know your mission:

- Every job becomes a stepping stone

- Every setback becomes a lesson

Your company has a backup plan.

Do you?

Start here:

→ List 3 skills you own (not rent)
→ Build one asset outside your job

The truth is:

Jobs are temporary.

Purpose is permanent.

MVPs aren't about building the perfect product.

HATE working with FAANG engineers in early days of startups.

Over the past 7+ years, I've managed 100+ engineers.

Some came from Google, Facebook, Microsoft.

Others came from failed startups, 3-person teams, garage companies.

FAANG engineers are optimization machines.
Startup engineers are survival machines.

Completely different skill sets.

FAANG engineer: We need to implement proper caching, set up monitoring, and plan for horizontal scaling.

Startup engineer: Let's ship this feature today and see if anyone uses it.

Real example from last month:

Client needed login system for their MVP.

FAANG engineer: Spent 2 weeks building OAuth integration, password encryption, session management, rate limiting.

Startup engineer: Used Firebase Auth, done in 2 hours.

Both work. 
One gets you funded. 
One gets you bankrupt.

FAANG engineers solve tomorrow's problems:
• What if we get 1M users?
• How do we handle peak traffic?
• What's our disaster recovery plan?

Startup engineers solve today's problems:
• Will users even sign up?
• Can we ship before runway ends?
• What's the fastest way to test this idea?

THE RULE:

Pre-product-market fit = Hire startup survivors
Post-product-market fit = Hire FAANG scalers

Wrong engineer at wrong stage = death.

Your 3-person startup doesn't need Google's infrastructure.

It needs to survive until product-market fit.

Build fast. Ship faster. Optimize later.

P.S. - I still love FAANG engineers. Just not at day 1 of your startup.
P.S.S - We have 100+ startup native engineers for your startup.

Whether you like it or NOT the most important decisions about your career are made when you’re not in the room.

Your title
Your pay
Your opportunities

All shaped in conversations you are not invited to.

As a former leader and exec I've sat in on them. 

Years ago, I was up for a promotion in another part of the business.

I had just started working with a new manager, and when I asked if she would support my candidacy, she said yes.

Later, I found out she told someone I was too junior for the role.

She didn’t stop the promotion, but she tried to.

What protected me was the brand I had already built.

And the advocates who already knew my work.

→ I led a process improvement project with measurable results
→ I built trust with leaders beyond my team
→ I earned visibility through recognition programs

That reputation made it easier for others to speak up.

And harder for her doubt to carry weight.

If you’ve ever had a manager say the right things in public but block you in private, you are not alone.

When I became a leader, I made it a point to speak up for my team.

Not just when it was easy, but especially when they were not in the room to speak for themselves.

Because I know what it feels like when someone stays silent or worse they don't protect you at all. 

If you want to protect your career, start here:

→ Be consistent
Show up the same way in high-stakes rooms and day-to-day meetings

→ Be clear
Speak in a way that leaves no one guessing what you do or why it matters

→ Be credible
Follow through on what you say, and let your results speak without over-explaining

Your boss or [insert here] may not be your biggest advocate.

But you can still have a strong campaign.

And when you become a leader, do not stay quiet in rooms where decisions are made.

Because if your name is going to be spoken, make sure it is by someone who protects it.

Who spoke your name when you weren’t in the room?

Tag them below and thank them.

Slapped a chatbot on a broken product.

original_post_text:
Founder: "We need leads NOW!"
Me: "Yes, but don't forget THIS principle:"

Marketing results are lagging indicators.

- Marketing tactics need time to generate results;
- Results come from decisions made weeks ago;
- They're linked to deeper strategic decisions.

In other words:

Marketing results reflect past decisions and efforts, not immediate changes.

So to "get leads", you'll need significant behind-the-scenes work:

- Customer research and offer design
- Positioning and messaging strategy
- Creating the right copy and design
- Publishing content on social media

But once you do it right, results flow.

Founder: "Ok, but why do our competitors get instant results?"
Me:

- You only see the end results: fame, revenue, growth
- You overlook the hours of experimentation invested
- You don't see the strategic decisions, like positioning

That's the Iceberg of Marketing.

Start focusing on the foundations.

Follow me, Pierre Herubel, for daily marketing tips.

I went from 0 clients to fully booked on LinkedIn in 6 months.

But if I had to start over from scratch?

Here’s what I’d do from Day 1 (and what I help my clients do now):

1) Pick ONE main offer to talk about.
2) Talk to 1 specific audience in every post.
3) Charge based on value and results, not fear.
4) Create at least one week of content in advance.
5) Try different formats to see which converts best.
6) Build a repeatable content system from week 1.
7) Track content performance weekly and adjust.
8) Prioritize engagement as much as posting.
9) Post with purpose and not just for likes.

Now my clients:

→ Get DMs from dream clients every week.
→ Have content that sells while they sleep.
→ Build trust without being online 24/7.

Visibility is great.

But if you want clients from LinkedIn, you need to be remembered - by the right people.

"I don't know how to do SEO"

Start from level 1 in The SEO Game:

To-do lists = Stressful

Game levels = Exhilarating

Reframe your SEO project as a game, and you'll become addicted to levelling up.

Here's my 9-level SEO game:

Level 1: Website Audit

- Crawl your website with Screaming Frog
- Analyse each page and assign actions (delete, 301...)
- Find 'quick win' keywords in GSC (position 3-20)
- Test your page speed in PageSpeed Insights

Level 2: Website Optimisation

- Execute technical actions from the audit
- Optimise your 'quick win' keywords from GSC
- Implement internal linking between key pages
- Create lead magnets for pages with existing traffic

Level 3: Competitor Analysis

- Identify your product and SEO competitors
- Enter their domain into Ahrefs (or similar)
- Study (and steal) their SEO strategies
- Conduct a 'gap analysis' vs your site

Level 4: Customer Research

- Send a survey to your customers with key questions
- Take 5 calls with power users to learn their pain points
- Collect customer research from the internet
- Create a document with all your findings

Level 5: Keyword Research

- Create a list of 'seed' keywords for further research
- Export all keywords from Ahrefs from your 'seeds'
- Import keywords in Keyword Insights for clustering
- Select topics to target in your content plan

Level 6: Content Production

- Start at the bottom funnel (comparisons, best of...)
- Move to the middle funnel (how-tos, templates...)
- Scale your top funnel (use Byword for programmatic)

Level 7: Backlink Acquisition

- Reach out to 10 influencers/websites for guest posts
- Create 3 linkable assets (statistics, infographics...)
- Submit your site to 10+ relevant directories/listings
- Track and monitor your backlink profile with Ahrefs

Level 8: Performance Tracking

- Ensure GA tracking and GSC are set up correctly
- Build a Looker Studio dashboard with this data
- Perform monthly competitor benchmarking
- Refine your SEO strategy based on this data

Level 9: Content Tuning

- Monitor your content and 'quick win' keywords
- Update content to better optimise for your topics
- Create lead magnets to convert traffic into emails
- Run A/B tests for CTAs to better convert traffic

Completed all levels?

Start again from level 1. The SEO Game never ends.

original_post_text:
met a 1st-time founder who raised a $17M Series A from a16z—in just 14 days. He had $3M cash & didn’t need the money— he cracked the fundraising game:

Marty got into YC with a VERY simple product. So simple YC said "it would never work". 

The product was not remarkable. But Marty and his co-founders were.

The 3 of them lived in their office full-time for months.

They spoke with 420 potential customers before building code.

They spoke 20+ founders before raising money.

And they absolutely CRACKED the fundraising game. 

After YC they raised a $3.2M round from General Catalyst in 6 days.

A year later, they raised a $17M Series A from a16z in 14 days.

They built a deck the night before the pitch. They didn't have a data room. 

They understood early-stage fundraising isn't about any of that.

"It's a social game...you have to be available, but not too available."

The booked 40 meetings in 2 weeks with investors.

They did it by getting other founders to reach out to VCs. 

But the subtleties are critical:

If a founder says "Pylon is raising a $17M Series A. Here's the deck. Do you want to meet?" You might get a meeting, but you get 0 urgency.

If a founder says "I'm good friends with Marty and heard they're raising. Have you met them yet? If not lmk, I can likely get you in." You'll get urgency.

I know—it all sounds silly. 

But in a world where people are constantly looking for the next big thing, they start to assume it must be hard to find, it must be highly desired, it must be "hot".

Of course, you need a solid team, a big market, and traction. That's still the 80%.

But if you want to raise a big round fast— you need urgency and momentum. 

Fundraising is a game.

So "we were playing the game as best as we could."

///
Get the exact playbook from Marty on The Product Market Fit Show.

remarks: This is a classic Case Study post. It uses a historical example (Wufoo) to lend authority. The contrast between low funding and massive ROI ($118k vs $35M) hooks the reader instantly. The Secret is a counter-intuitive management tactic that creates a strong narrative of common sense vs. industry norms.
original_post_text:
In 2011, a tiny YC startup called Wufoo was acquired for $35 million.

They had raised only $118,000 in funding.

Their return to investors? 29,000%.

While everyone else was following the "raise millions and burn cash" playbook.

Wufoo's three founders built a company that defied startup logic:

-  10 employees total
-  No office (everyone remote)
-  Profitable within 9 months
-  500,000+ users at acquisition
-  Support Response time of 7-12 MINUTES

The secret?
 
They made their ENGINEERS do CUSTOMER SUPPORT.

Every Wufoo engineer, including the founders, spent one FULL day every week doing customer support.

- The engineers initially hated it.
- The investors thought it was insane.

But here's what happened:

→ Engineers fixed bugs immediately after answering the same question 3 times, engineers would stop everything and fix the root cause.

→ Engineers built features users actually needed (not what looked good in demos)

→ They redesigned their documentation ONCE and support tickets dropped 30% THE NEXT DAY

→ When users struggled with a feature, developers would simplify it instead of explaining it

Simply put: Engineers who talk to customers build different products.

They optimize for user experience, not technical elegance.

The lesson from Wufoo's $35M exit isn't complicated.

Remove the barriers between the people who build the product and the people who use it.

Want to build something people love? DM me I can help you.

original_post_text:
just dropped $320 at a store that has no selection, no ads, and no consistency.

A bunch of you did too — these guys made $237 billion last year.

I'm talking about Costco. This company took the retail bible, tore it to shreds, then lit it on fire.

Here's what they did next.


1/ Members Only

No card? Costco will shamelessly turn you away at the door. You gotta flash that plastic if you want to wander these orange aisles. And for good reason. 

Costco’s membership is perhaps the most brilliant business maneuver of the last century.

First off, it generated $4.6B last year. Almost 75% of the company's profit.

It provides them with a cash windfall to fund operations.

I get my membership fee back each year through my 2% rebate. Lots of people do. But even then, we've just loaned Costco $120 for 12 months at 0% interest.

Well done Costco. Please take my money.


2/ Zero selection

Looking for ketchup? We got you. I mean, as long as you're good with 2 mini-barrels of Heinz.

What's that? It's Mac & Cheese you're after?

All good, here's a 28-pound bucket. Hope you worked those triceps. 

There's a reason Costco offers 1-2 product options and no more. It's because you buy more of it.

8 different varieties of pickle might sound good, but then you get lost in choice and buy nothing.

If selection is your vibe, Costco doesn't have it. But if you want an unreasonable number of Wasabi Doritos, you know where to find them.


3/ Secret Giant

Costco isn't just a retail juggernaut, it's also a consumer product giant.

Their in house brand, Kirkland Signature made a ridiculous $58B last year. This puts them in the big leagues next to Unilever and P&G. And way ahead of lightweights like Hershey and Kellogg.

You know how much Kirkland spends on advertising?

Of course you do. Nothing.

Kirkland makes batteries, cashews, toilet paper, wine. Whatever you need. As good or better than the big brands.


4/ Costco Vibes

There's so much more to cover:

• the endless samples, from bagel bites to cheese perogies

• the no-questions-asked return policy

• the hot dog and soda combo for $1.50

• the $21K bottles of scotch

It's a vibe. And Costco does it well.


5/ My take

Costco leans into its brand. Hard.

When you do one or two weird things, it looks strange. Maybe inauthentic. But when you do everything different, the questions stop. People either love you or hate you.

Buy in 100%. Others will follow.

Costco invented its own model of retail by rewriting the playbook.

No half-steps. No hesitation.

Costco is playing its own game.

P.S - [Call to Action/Lead Magnet Link].
remarks: This 'Work-Life Reframe' works by admitting to a 'guilty' behavior (not working 8 hours) that the reader secretly desires. It uses the 'Brain Screams' section to validate the reader's anxiety, making the advice feel empathetic. The 3-point breakdown uses arrows to show direct causality between rest and result.
original_post_text:
I rarely sit at my desk for 8 hours a day.

I’ve found it doesn’t help me be the most productive.

So instead, these are 3 non-negotiables I have:

1/ Go for a walk
2/ Take a long lunch break
3/ Finish and have the evening to relax

Every time I follow these, my brain screams “You should be working.”
But over the last 4 years, I’ve learned that voice isn’t always right.

There’s a big difference between being busy, and being productive.
You can sit at a desk for 10 hours and achieve very little.

Or you can work for 4 focused hours and get more done than most do in a week.

I’ve found:

→ Walking clears your head and sparks better ideas
→ Long lunches give your brain space to process and create
→ Evenings off help you recharge so you can go again tomorrow

The truth is:

You rarely need to work longer days.
You rarely need to “hustle” more.
You rarely need to work later.

Working hard helps, but only when you're working on the right thing. 

Being productive > being busy for the sake of being busy. 

That’s what builds a sustainable business that you love waking up to.

P.S - I compiled my strategy (how I grew my brand to 23,000+ followers and built a business from it) into a free playbook. It’s free to access here: https://lnkd.in/eNBJy5Ky

original_post_text:
I hit quota 30 months straight as a SDR. 

Here’s the blueprint.

Most SDRs miss quota at some point. I refused to let that happen.

Here are the 7 things that made the difference:

𝟭. 𝗖𝗼𝗹𝗱 𝗰𝗮𝗹𝗹 𝗱𝗮𝗶𝗹𝘆: 
Reps who don’t call don’t book meetings. I made calls every day, no excuses.

𝟮. 𝗣𝗶𝗽𝗲𝗹𝗶𝗻𝗲 𝗼𝘃𝗲𝗿 𝗽𝗲𝗿𝗳𝗲𝗰𝘁𝗶𝗼𝗻:
The best SDRs don’t wait for the perfect message. More touchpoints = more meetings. Quantity fuels quality.

𝟯. 𝗙𝗼𝗹𝗹𝗼𝘄 𝘂𝗽 𝗹𝗶𝗸𝗲 𝘆𝗼𝘂𝗿 𝗹𝗶𝗳𝗲 𝗱𝗲𝗽𝗲𝗻𝗱𝘀 𝗼𝗻 𝗶𝘁: 
80% of my meetings were booked after the 5th follow-up. If you give up after 1-2 attempts, you're leaving opportunities on the table.

𝟰. 𝗟𝗲𝗮𝗱 𝘄𝗶𝘁𝗵 𝘁𝗵𝗲 𝗽𝗿𝗼𝗯𝗹𝗲𝗺, 𝗻𝗼𝘁 𝘁𝗵𝗲 𝗽𝗶𝘁𝗰𝗵: 
Nobody cares about your product. They care about their pain. Start with what’s hurting them, then guide them to the solution.

𝟱. 𝗕𝗲 𝘀𝗼 𝗰𝗼𝗻𝗳𝗶𝗱𝗲𝗻𝘁, 𝘁𝗵𝗲𝘆 𝗮𝘀𝘀𝘂𝗺𝗲 𝘆𝗼𝘂’𝗿𝗲 𝗿𝗶𝗴𝗵𝘁:
Your tone sells as much as your words. Sound like a pro, and they’ll treat you like one. Your voice = your credibility.

𝟲. 𝗧𝗿𝗮𝗰𝗸 𝗲𝘃𝗲𝗿𝘆𝘁𝗵𝗶𝗻𝗴: 
I measured calls made, emails sent, meetings booked, and conversion rates. What gets tracked gets improved.

𝟳. 𝗕𝘂𝗶𝗹𝗱 𝗮 𝘀𝘁𝗿𝗼𝗻𝗴 𝗿𝗲𝗹𝗮𝘁𝗶𝗼𝗻𝘀𝗵𝗶𝗽 𝘄𝗶𝘁𝗵 𝘆𝗼𝘂𝗿 𝗔𝗘: 
This might be the most underrated key to success. Your AE technically OWNS your quota. The better your relationship, the more they’ll have your back. 

My AEs saw how hard I fought to bring them good opportunities, so when I was falling short, they helped, passing me leads or even crediting me on deals they sourced themselves. 

It made all the difference.

VCs will HATE this post.

I'm about to DESTROY the Silicon Valley myth.

The VC playbook has brainwashed a generation of founders:

- Raise millions
- Burn cash
- Scale at all costs
- Figure out profit "later"

But here's what they don't want you to know:

94% of unicorns NEVER reach profitability.

After working with 100+ startups, I've seen behind the curtain:

VCs aren't investing in YOUR success. 

They're playing a numbers game where YOU are the poker chip.

The math is brutal:

- For every 10 investments, 7-8 fail completely
- 1-2 return the original investment
- They need ONE massive exit to make the fund work

They NEED you to take existential risks.

Meanwhile, the most successful founders I've worked with ignored the Silicon Valley playbook entirely:

- Bootstrapped their first $1-3M ARR 
- Reached profitability within 18 months 
- Only raised money to ACCELERATE proven models 
- Retained majority ownership at exit

One founder we worked with turned down a $3M seed round.

Instead, he:

- Built an MVP for $30K
- Charged customers from DAY ONE
- Reached $1.2M ARR without a single investor

While his VC-backed competitors:

- Burned through $50M collectively
- Failed to find sustainable unit economics
- Gave away 85%+ of their companies
- Are now desperately trying to avoid bankruptcy

The truth VCs won't tell you:

Outside Silicon Valley, 99% of successful businesses NEVER raise venture capital.

Your business doesn't need to:

- Grow 20% month-over-month
- Reach $100M ARR
- Disrupt entire industries

It needs to SOLVE REAL PROBLEMS and make more money than it spends.

Don't sacrifice your company on the altar of hypergrowth.

Build a REAL business instead.

remarks: This 'Patience Playbook' works by normalizing delayed gratification. It validates the frustration of slow growth while positioning it as a competitive advantage. The bulleted list of 'what impatient people get wrong' acts as a mirror, helping the reader identify their own anxieties and choose the more strategic path.
original_post_text:
Great marketing doesn't feel like marketing.

And to do great marketing, you have to be willing to play the long game.

You have to expect that someone might follow you for months before visiting your website.

That they might read your newsletter for a year before becoming a customer.

That they might share your posts 10 times before buying anything.

Stay the course. Here's what impatient marketers get wrong:

• They think building trust takes too long
• They get anxious when content doesn't drive immediate clicks
• They switch tactics before momentum builds

The "slow" path is more sustainable. Because when someone finally does convert, they're not just a customer. They're an advocate.

Stop rushing the relationship. Start playing the long game.

name: The Vulnerable Launch Story
description: This format works by admitting a deep, relatable fear (failure/rejection) right before announcing a success or product launch. It shifts the narrative from selling to serving, making the ultimate CTA feel earned rather than pushed.
original_post_text:
3 weeks ago… I almost didn’t do it.

That familiar voice in my head whispered again:

“What if no one signs up?”
“What if it flops... again?”

Because truth be told...
my past experience with webinars was painful.

I’d spend weeks preparing.
Writing. Rehearsing. 
Promoting on Eventbright.

And then...
3 signups. Maybe 5.
A couple silent squares on Zoom.

When it ended, I’d close my laptop…
and just sit there.
Feeling like I failed.

So when the idea for this new workshop came up,
I almost said no.

Because I didn’t want to feel that disappointment again.

But then I asked myself a harder question:

“What if this time, it’s not about me?”
“What if I make it entirely about THEM?”

That single shift changed everything.

I stopped trying to sell.
And started trying to SERVE.

I poured 5 years of trial, error, breakthroughs into one 2-hour experience..

The kind of workshop I wish someone had given me
when I was stuck, overthinking, and staying silent.

I called it:
🟣 From Silent to Seen: For mid-senior professionals ready to speak with authority and own their presence.

- No tricks.
- No gimmicks.
- No “fake it till you make it.”

→ Just science-based insights
→ Psychology-backed tools.
→ And a pathway to real transformation.

And this time… something WILD happened.

No ads.
No fancy funnels.
No shouting into the void.

Just value.

And people showed up.
Fast.
So fast… 

the workshop sold out 1.5 weeks before the event.
(it's a free event.. for now)

I originally planned for 20 spots.
But had to expand to 28.

That moment.. watching it fill up hit me hard.

Because it wasn’t just a “business win.”
It’s proof.

Proof that when you stop performing and start serving, 
people feel it.

They don’t just want more content.
They want transformation.
They want truth.

And that’s what I plan to deliver on November 9th.

A live, intimate, no-fluff workshop
where every single person leaves with a pathway to confidence that lasts.

So the doors are officially CLOSED for this event.

I’ve got a lot of rehearsal ahead because: 

Dale Carnegie said:
“Only the prepared speaker deserves to be confident.”

And I want this to be more than a workshop..
A pathway to your transformation.

But if you missed this one and want to be part of the next..

👉 DM me “WAITLIST” 

...and I’ll add you to early access for next one.

name: The Boundary Call-Out
description: This format works by addressing a negative experience (like being copied or slighted) without anger. It turns a potential rant into an educational 'teachable moment,' establishing the author as an unbothered industry leader.
original_post_text:
3+ years on LinkedIn.

Never seen anything like this...

This morning, I checked my profile visits.

One profile instantly grabbed my attention.

That tagline.

Is that my story?

Damn.

It is.

I checked this guy's profile and saw:

- My banner on his profile.
- My background story as his tagline.

WTF. 

I've been on LinkedIn for 3+ years.

Seen people copying my content.

Totally fine with that.

But trying to copy my profile.

Next level.

So I sent this guy a message.

Not to threaten him, but to tell him there are better ways to grow on LinkedIn. 

I told him to:

1. Be authentic.
Copying posts, or using A to speed things up, that's fine. Your profile? That should be 100% yours. Not a copy.

2. Learn from others.
Instead of copying. Follow people like Justin Welsh, Lara Acosta, Nick Broekema, and Daniel Korenblum. Understand how to master LinkedIn and apply those learnings.

3. Be visible. 
Start being visible on LinkedIn, not by copying, but by adding value.

At the end of the day. 

We all copy from each other.

That's great.

I do it too.

But always give it your own twist. 

Never just copy + paste.

And please let's keep our profile authentic.

Deal?

Hi, I'm Roelof Otten. I post 5x a week about Product-Led Growth and LinkedIn so SaaS founders can grow their business.

57 LinkedIn connection requests sent.
46 accepted. 22 replied.
1 deal closed in 3 weeks.

(and I didn't spam a single person)

Here's what changed everything for me:

I stopped chasing random people.
I started finding leads who already care about what I can solve.

Think about it like this:

You wouldn't walk into a coffee shop and pitch everyone inside.
You'd find the people reading books about your topic.
You'd sit next to those discussing your industry.
You'd talk to those asking questions you can answer.

That's exactly what I do on LinkedIn.

I've tested this exact systems for 2 years.

Here's my 5-step process:

→ Find 3 of my old posts with real discussions (not just likes)
→ Check competitor posts for similar engaged audiences
→ Search live events related to my solution
→ Look for cohorts or bootcamps in my space
→ Push all leads through n8n for qualification

Then I filter by:
- Location
- Company size
- Industry fit

Now I have 57 people who have already shown interest.

These aren't cold leads.
They're problem-aware or solution-aware.

Once I get the list, I move them into Aimfox and run a simple campaign:

1. Send them a free resource (related to what they engaged with)
2. Ask for feedback
3. Once they respond, ask, "What's stopping you from solving this?"
4. Share how I solved this for a previous client
5. Book the call

Results from one campaign:

✅ 5 calls booked
✅ 1 deal closed
✅ 4 high-quality leads in pipeline

Speed beats perfection here.

If one campaign doesn't work?
Test another angle.
Try a different resource.
Adjust your message.

Finding leads has never been easier.
And problem-aware audiences are the best place to start.

Let me know in the comments if you want my prompt that will help you to qualify your leads.

6 steps to reclaim 15+ hours every week with AI.

Most founders spend hours each week on tasks that could be:

→ Delegated.
→ Automated.
→ Restructured.

... with the help of AI.

And it adds up.

10 minutes here, 45 minutes there.

Before you know it, half your week is gone.

Here’s the 6-step process I use to help founders redesign their week and reclaim 15+ hours with AI:

Step 1: Audit your week.

Write down every recurring task for 7 days straight.

→ Emails.
→ Invoices.
→ Scheduling.
→ Drafting content.

You’ll be shocked at what fills your time when you see it in black and white.

Step 2: Replace admin first.

Start with the “low-hanging fruit.” Tools like Claude and Zapier can handle:

→ Follow-ups.
→ Inbox triage.
→ Meeting prep.
→ Scheduling.

These alone free 3–5 hours a week.

Step 3: Repurpose your content.

Stop creating from scratch. Turn one LinkedIn post into:

→ A thread.
→ A video.
→ An email.

... all using AI. This multiplies your presence while cutting content time by 70%.

Step 4: Integrate into workflows.

Don’t let AI sit as a separate tool. Connect it directly to where you already work:

→ Slack.
→ Your CRM.
→ Google Drive.

That way, it’s embedded into your daily flow.

Step 5: Measure time saved.

Track hours reclaimed. You need to see the ROI of AI, or it just feels like “playing with tools.”

Most founders hit 15+ hours freed in the first month.

Step 6: Expand gradually.

Once admin and content are optimized, move into delivery and client experience.

AI can:

→ Analyze feedback.
→ Personalize onboarding.
→ Draft proposals.

Follow this loop:

Audit → Automate → Measure → Expand.

This is exactly the type of system we build (and stress-test) together inside the Lab.

name: The Matrix Taxonomy
description: This format works by taking a confusing, overwhelming topic (like 'Which AI tool do I use?') and organizing it into a clear, relatable matrix or taxonomy. It positions the author as a master conductor of tools rather than just a user.
original_post_text:
90% of people = Same generic prompts everywhere

10% who understand = 10x better results

I spent 732 days burning through AI subscriptions.
Testing every tool. Breaking every feature.
Because immigrant mathematics taught me: Wrong tool = Wasted time.

I watched a founder last week copy-paste the same prompt into 4 different tools.
Got 4 mediocre outputs.
"AI isn't that smart," he said.

No. You're using a hammer for brain surgery.

Here's what Big4 consulting taught me about tools:
You don't use Excel for presentations.
You don't use PowerPoint for data analysis.
Same logic applies to AI.

CHATGPT → Your patient instructor
Talk to it like a teacher who needs context
"Explain this concept with examples."
"Break down this complex topic."
Perfect for: Learning, explaining, creating educational content

PERPLEXITY → Your fact-checking analyst
Treat it like your research department
"Find recent data on..."
"What are the latest trends in..."
Perfect for: Market research, competitive analysis, verified facts

GROK → Your brutally honest friend
The one who tells you what others won't
Direct. Unfiltered. Real-time X data.
"What's the real sentiment about..."
Perfect for: Social listening, trend spotting, reality checks

GEMINI → Your strategic project manager
Speaks Google's language fluently
"Create a project plan for..."
"Optimize this for search..."
Perfect for: SEO content, Google ecosystem integration, structured planning

The plot twist nobody sees:

While everyone's asking "Which AI is best?"
You're asking "Which AI for THIS specific task?"

My 4-step system for AI tool mastery:

1. Match the tool to the task
→ Need facts? Perplexity
→ Need creativity? ChatGPT
→ Need real-time social data? Grok
→ Need Google integration? Gemini

2. Speak their language
→ ChatGPT: Conversational, detailed context
→ Perplexity: Specific queries, data-focused
→ Grok: Direct questions, no fluff
→ Gemini: Structured requests, project-oriented

3. Layer your approach
→ Research with Perplexity first
→ Develop ideas with ChatGPT
→ Validate sentiment with Grok
→ Structure output with Gemini

4. Document what works
→ Save your best prompts per tool
→ Build your prompt library
→ Iterate based on results

Because here's the thing:

Your competitors are still debating IF they should use AI.
You're mastering WHICH AI to use WHEN.

That gap? That's your competitive moat.

The immigrant mathematics is simple:
Right tool + Right prompt = Exponential results
Wrong tool + Generic prompt = Wasted potential

Stop treating AI like one thing.
Start treating it like an orchestra.

Different instruments. Different sounds.
Master conductor? Unstoppable symphony.

💭 Which AI tool have you been using wrong this whole time?

♻️ Share for someone still copy-pasting the same prompt everywhere
➕ Follow Anna Bilan ⚡️ for AI strategies that actually work

description: This format builds extreme trust by openly admitting to chasing vanity metrics and feeling miserable despite 'succeeding.' It then pivots to a healthier, more authentic system that prioritizes quality over quantity.
original_post_text:
I figured out how to go viral on LinkedIn.

It worked too well. And I almost quit.

A few months ago, my reach dropped by 40-50%.

And like most people on LinkedIn… I panicked 😱

So I started experimenting:

– Shorter posts
– Punchier lines
– Simpler thoughts

And it worked:

✅ 241k impressions
✅ 71k impressions
✅ 81k impressions
✅ 62k impressions

📈 +1,700 followers in September alone.

But the metrics don’t show what I lost:

– My ego got louder.
– I checked stats constantly
– I stopped enjoying LinkedIn
– I spent more time here, not less

The worst part?

Eventually, 80% of my posts were reach-chasers.

At the end of September, I had to face it:

The ego (the silent assassin) was back.

So I decided to build a new system.

A content calendar with one rule:

✅ Only one “reach” post per week
✅ The rest? Posts that are my authentic reflections
✅ All focused on connection, not visibility

And just like that, everything changed:

My engagement rate climbed from 4% to 12%.

My mental clarity returned

I started loving LinkedIn again

Yes, reach dropped. 
But joy came back.

Just like it used to feel when I started 12 months ago.

Feels good to be back.

Happy Monday out there everyone! 🖤

name: The Zero to Hero Retrospective
description: A comprehensive listicle framed as a look back from a point of success. It contrasts the struggle of 'Day 1' with the current reality, delivering a high volume of rapid-fire insights.
original_post_text:
I gave up on LinkedIn at least 10 times.

Today? I just hit 18,000 followers.

But it didn’t start that way.

12 months ago.

I had:

No traction.
No momentum.
No inbound leads.

And no clue how to turn content into clients.

I was even scared I’d have to go back to outbound.

Now my content brings in 100+ leads per week.

Because I stopped writing for attention.

And started writing to shift beliefs.

If I had to start from 0 all over again…

These are the 18 insights I’d build around 👇

 1. Create gravity — don’t chase leads.
 2. Build systems. Charisma doesn’t scale.
 3. Relevance > reach. Speak to one person.
 4. Great content mirrors your reader’s reality.
 5. Epiphanies collapse time — and resistance.
 6. The real funnel is emotional: curiosity → trust.
 7. You don’t need more effort. You need leverage.
 8. When they say “that’s me” — they’re already sold.
 9. The clearest offer always wins — not the cheapest.
10. You’re not selling tips — you’re selling understanding.
11. Insight + relevance beats “value” every single time.
12. Trust > urgency. Let people feel safe saying yes.
13. Don’t chase tactics. Install one clear narrative.
14. DMs aren’t cold if your content built the trust.
15. Conviction converts faster than persuasion.
16. Every post should earn the right for a DM.
17. Reframing beats teaching — every time.
18. Attention without belief is just noise.

Save this.

Open it on the days you want to quit.

Your next client might be one belief shift away.

Someone out there needs your message.

Let them find you.

So keep showing up.

Keep writing.

They’re closer than you think.

P.S. Which insight hit hardest? 👇

name: The Value vs. Insight Pivot
description: This format deconstructs a common piece of advice ('give away free value') and explains why it fails to convert. It then introduces a higher-level concept ('Insight') that actually drives sales.
original_post_text:
I posted on LinkedIn for 6 years and got 0 leads.

Until I changed one thing.

Not my service.
Not my pricing.
Not my niche.

What I changed?

The type of content I shared.

For years, I thought value = leads.
So I posted tips.
Tactics.
How-to’s.
I shared my entire playbook.

People said:

“Thanks!”
“Saving this!”
“I’ll try it out!”

But no one bought.

Because value makes people think:

“Cool info.”

Insight makes them say:

“I need to talk to this person.”

Here’s the shift that changed everything:

Step 1: Stop trying to educate.

Your content shouldn’t feel like a free course.

It should create clarity.

Epiphanies.

A new way of thinking.

If they see the problem differently...
They’ll see you differently too.

Step 2: Share insights, not instructions.

Teach them what to believe—not just what to do.

Example:
❌ “Here are 3 DM frameworks”
✅ “Here’s why your DMs aren’t working, and how to fix it.”

See the difference?

Step 3: Talk to one person.

Most content tries to reach everyone.
That’s why it reaches no one.

Write to the one person you actually want to help.
The right person will feel like you’re reading their mind.

That’s when they DM you.
That’s when they buy.

Now?

→ I get inbound leads weekly.
→ Clients pay high-ticket without pressure.
→ And I don’t need to go viral to make it happen.

All because I stopped sharing value…
And started sharing insight.

P.S.

Inside Inner Circle—my private community for agency owners, coaches—we show you how to build a system that makes this repeatable.

→ The right content
→ For the right person
→ Delivered in a way that makes selling feel easy

We close in 6 days.

DM me “6IC” if you want an invite.

name: The Hidden Motivation
description: This format challenges a widely accepted belief about why people do things (like leaving jobs) and replaces it with a deeper, more emotional truth. It works exceptionally well for leadership and culture topics.
original_post_text:
I used to think people left jobs for more money

(I was wrong)

They’re not chasing bigger paychecks.
They’re chasing peace of mind.

They leave when:

- Effort is never enough.
- Silence feels safer than honesty.
- Burnout is expected and vulnerability is a liability.

So here’s a reminder from Dan: 
Retention starts with culture.

If you want to retain your best people? 

Start with respect.

That means:

→ Managers who support, not shame
→ A culture where it’s safe to be honest
→ Teams that value rest as much as results

The paycheck is important, sure. 

But it’s not the reason they stay.

Do you agree?

♻️ More people need to see this, share it with your network!

Follow Rob Dance for more posts like this.

name: The Patience Reframe
description: A vulnerable post that normalizes the 'sucking phase' of any new endeavor. It shifts the blame from external factors (the algorithm, the niche) to the internal process and patience.
original_post_text:
I used to think the problem was my writing.
 
Turns out, the problem was my patience.
 
I’d post once, watch it flop,
and immediately assume I was trash.
 
Then I’d spiral...
 
“Maybe I’m not made for this.”
“Maybe people just don’t care about it.”
“Maybe I should just… repost quotes and call it a day.”
 
But here’s what no one tells you:
 
- It’s not the algorithm.
- It’s not your niche.
- It’s your process.
 
Everyone wants to go viral.
Almost no one wants to be bad first.
 
But that’s how it works.
 
That’s how you build the muscle.
That’s how you earn clarity, flow, and trust.
 
In 2025, I didn’t go viral on LinkedIn.
 
I practiced consistency.
And that changed everything.
 
Because the people who look effortless online?
 
They’re just the ones who stayed through the awkward
phase long enough to stop caring who was watching.

name: The Platform Independence Playbook
description: This format advises against relying on third-party platforms and champions building a personal 'identity' or brand. It uses a strong hypothetical 'start over' hook.
original_post_text:
If I had to start freelancing again from zero,
I wouldn’t open a single marketplace account.

Not Fiverr.
Not Upwork.
Not even LinkedIn Jobs.

After 7 years in the field, I learned one painful truth:

You can lose your account.
You can lose your job.
But you can’t lose your identity.

And that’s the real business.

Most freelancers learn a skill and rush to create a profile.
Or take an internship just to “get experience.”

But the stable ones?
They build identity.

Your identity is what makes people remember you.
It’s what makes clients reach out to you, not the other way around.

Think of it like this:

→ Skill is your tool
→ Marketplace is your workshop
→ Identity is your brand

Lose the first two, and you still survive with the third.
Because when people know you, your ROI compounds.

P.S. What exactly is “identity”? Check the comments.

name: The Invisible Enemy
description: Identifies a deep psychological barrier (like imposter syndrome) and methodically deconstructs it, turning an emotional problem into a logical one that can be solved.
original_post_text:
Imposter syndrome will kill your career.

Here's what nobody will tell you:

Every time you think:

"I'm just NOT good enough..."
"I could never do..."
"That's not who I am..."

Your brain is lying to you.

Remember the last time you had to speak in a meeting?
Or share an idea with someone important?
Or challenge someone's view?

That voice in your head probably said:
→ "Stay quiet"
→ "Play it safe"
→ "Don't attract attention"

Sound familiar?

Here's the truth nobody talks about on Linkedin:

That voice? Those limitations?
They're NOT facts.
They're just beliefs.

And BELIEFS?

They're just thoughts you've repeated so many times
your brain thinks they're facts.

Let me show you how deep this goes:

1. Beliefs run your life (secretly)
↳ Shape how you show up in meetings
↳ Control what opportunities you take
↳ Decide if you speak up or stay silent

2. Beliefs are stories you inherited
↳ From that teacher who said "be quiet"
↳ From that time you stumbled on stage
↳ From watching others play small

3. Beliefs create evidence
↳ Think you're "not good enough"?
↳ Your brain will find proof
↳ Even when it's NOT true

4. Beliefs are protective lies
↳ Designed to keep you "safe"
↳ Built from old wounds
↳ But blocking your growth today

My entire life..

I acted SHY and SMALL
Because I used to believe:

• I was born this way
• It's in my DNA
• I'm socially awkward...

And I will die this way.

But turned out... 
all that was a BIG FAT LIE.

And here's what I know about you:

→ You're not "just shy"
→ You're not "naturally anxious"
→ You're not "bad at speaking"

These are stories you've been hearing and telling yourself

And stories?
They can be rewritten.

name: The Lifestyle Design Story
description: A deeply personal narrative that contrasts a chaotic, profit-driven past with a peaceful, system-driven present. It proves authority through lifestyle rather than just revenue.
original_post_text:
In 2019, I moved back to Cyprus after 9 years in London.

I didn’t come back for a business opportunity.
I came back because my dad was sick.

I wanted to be close.
And I figured… I’d find my way somehow.

That first year?
I made $12,000. Total.

I had zero strategy or vision.
I didn't have an online audience either.
I was throwing spaghetti at the wall, trying to figure out how to make something work online.

Scared.
Clueless.
Determined.

Fast forward to now:

I work 3 days a week.
I’m home with my wife and daughter.
I walk my dogs. I run. I play music.
I sit in cafés with a handful of friends.
And I watch the seasons not change, because Cyprus gives me summer on loop.

The biggest shift?

I stopped chasing big.
And I started repeating simple.

One post.
One offer.
One email.
Every day.

(🚨 ps. I wrote a 30 page book outlining each step of the model. Join 6,100+ legends who got their copy: https://lnkd.in/dtwPYyqf - it’s free)

That’s what got me here:

→ 7 figures profit.
→ 50,000 followers.
→ 6,000 people on my list.
→ 200+ incredible clients that inspire me.

Not because I cracked a code.
But because I kept showing up long enough to find my rhythm.

Now?

If there’s any secret to this life, it’s this:

Stop trying to be the best.

original_post_text:
Age 18: My dad sent $560 for university tuition from Pakistan.

Age 18: I bought a Facebook page about Japanese swords instead.

I thought I'd be a millionaire in 6 months.

Age 18: Sold my first sword for $50.

Age 19-20: Made $17,000 selling 250+ swords.

Bought my first car. Felt like I conquered the world.

Age 21: Facebook reach started tanking.

Posts that got 500 likes now got 50. I had no idea why.

Started learning sales the hard way.
Cold messaging people. 
Testing different offers.
Figuring out what actually made people buy.

Age 22 Revenue dropped to $500/month.

The algorithm changed. I didn't adapt fast enough.

Age 22: Facebook suspended the page permanently.

Gone. All of it.

I was making $500/month.
Back where I started.
Except now I had a car payment.

Age 22: I realize I didn't want to sell swords.

I wanted to build software.

Started Softaims as a freelancer.

Just me. My laptop. Client projects for $100 first project

Age 23: Got my first office space.

Tiny. Shared

Started renting. Started thinking bigger.

Age 24: Made the dumbest mistake of my life.

Revenue was decent. I thought: "I need a team to scale."

So I hired 10 people. At once.

No systems.
No processes.
No business structure.

All revenue was under my personal name.

Age 25: Payroll was $20,000/month.

Revenue was $15,000/month.

Do the math. I was bleeding cash.

Half the team didn't know what to do. 
The other half were doing work we couldn't sell.

Age 25-28: 

Struggled for 3 years straight.

Start getting all business on compnay Softaims

Built actual systems. Sales processes. Delivery frameworks.

But the damage was done. 
Reputation hurt. 
Bank account empty.

Not "broke" struggling. Worse.

Making just enough to stay alive but not enough to grow.

I couldn't figure out why we'd win one deal and lose the next five.

Age 27: Finally learned sales and marketing properly.

Stopped guessing. Started testing.

Learned how to:

-  Actually qualify leads
-  Position our services correctly
-  Close without being desperate
-  Deliver results that got referrals

Age 28: Revenue stabilized. Then grew.

We hit $30K months consistently.

Then $50K months.

Then $80K months.

Age 28-29: Built the team the right way this time.

Hired slowly. 
Trained properly. 
Had systems first, people second.

Realized we were better at helping software companies than anyone else.

Today: Portfolio of 53+ startups we've helped scale.

Combined value: $138M+.

Team: Profitable and growing.

Me: Still learning. Still making mistakes. Still figuring it out.

name: The Bright Side / Dark Side Contrast
description: Contrasts a successful process against a failing one. Excellent for highlighting the importance of foundations over surface-level tactics.
original_post_text:
Every product launch can have two opposing sides:

The bright side:
 ✨ Clear ICP
 ✨ Sharp positioning
 ✨ Sales and support trained
 ✨ Messaging locked in
 ✨ Seamless internal + external rollout

The dark side:
 💀 “Everyone = audience”
 💀 Random channel selection
 💀 One and done promo checklist
 💀 Customer success is sent a Slack memo
 💀 No pricing alignment with sales

The difference?

The foundations.

They start by getting the core right:
→ In your ICP, your value prop, your distribution strategy.

Access to more budget, resources, and creative license can amplify a good launch into greatness...but will crumble if you're spraying and praying. 

Also, a litmus test of a well-planned launch in B2B SaaS is when sales and CS move in sync and can answer:

"Why should a customer care about this feature?"
"What is this launch about?" 
"What's the 1-line tldr here?"

Choose your side wisely.

name: The Invisible Cage
description: A deep psychological teardown of a successful client's internal struggles. Builds massive authority for coaching and consulting.
original_post_text:
He spoke at Harvard, gave keynotes, media appearances

Yet every time he spoke publicly, a quiet voice whispered: 

“Do I really belong here?”

For most of his life, he carried a belief.
Not loud. 
Not obvious.
A whisper that slowly became his truth.

“You’re not good enough.”

And that whisper shaped EVERYTHING.

He’d done what most high achievers do:
→ Made millions
→ Collected titles
→ Earned degrees

Checked all the boxes ✅ 

But deep inside, he still felt:
Something's missing.

He’d hold back in conversations.
Avoid the spotlight.
Shrink... even in rooms he'd already earned his way into.

Then he began working with me.

At first, he thought it was about:
Voice. 
Delivery.
Practicing.

But very quickly, he realized:
This wasn’t about technique... It was about TRUTH.

We went DEEP...
→ Uncovered beliefs he didn’t even know he’d agreed to.
→ Inherited STORIES from teachers, parents, painful moments.

And then.. something shifted.

One day, he walked into a talk with no notes.
No scripts.
No armor.

There was tension, yes.
But also… peace.
Presence.

And for the first time, he felt FREE.

He told me later:

“I realized I had been the sculptor of my own cage.
And if I built it, I could also dismantle it.”

That realization changed EVERYHTING.

He started showing up differently.
Speaking differently.
Thinking differently.

Not fake confidence.
Not performance.
But deep, unshakable presence.

He said,

“This wasn’t just coaching.
It was reconstruction.. of my identity.
I feel elevated in a way I haven’t felt in decades.
I’m not a creation of others’ opinions anymore.
I’m the creator of my path.”

And that’s the real transformation.
• Not louder speech.
• But liberated voice.
• Not just better communication.
• But rewired identity.

Because at the end of the day:

It’s never about what others do to you.. 
It’s what you do for yourself.

And he chose to rise.

Every.
Single.
Time.

original_post_text:
How to hire a dev shop that won't screw you over:

(From someone who's seen both sides)

After working with 100+ clients and competing against 1000+ dev shops, here's the checklist:

RED FLAGS 🚩

→ They promise "anything is possible" 
→ They don't ask about your business goals 
→ They give you a price in the first call 
→ Their portfolio has 50+ "successful projects" 
→ They want 100% payment upfront 
→ They won't do a paid test project
→ They push you to add MORE features

GREEN FLAGS ✅:

→ They ask "why are you building this?" 
→ They challenge your assumptions 
→ They show you similar projects they've done 
→ They're transparent about limitations
→ They suggest starting small 
→ They talk about POST-launch support 
→ They have verifiable client testimonials

*The questions YOU should ask:

1. "Can I talk to 3 of your recent clients?"
2. "What happens if you miss the deadline?"
3. "Who owns the code?"
4. "What's your communication process?"
5. "Have you ever told a client NOT to build something?"

That last question is CRITICAL.

If they've never said no to a client, they're just taking your money.

The best dev shops will: 
→ Save you money 
→ Challenge bad ideas 
→ Protect you from yourself

We've walked away from $500K+ in projects because we knew they'd fail.

That's the partner you want.

Not someone who will build anything for a check.

Questions? Drop them below.

PS: At Softaims Dev, we have a "honesty-first" policy. If your idea won't work, we'll tell you before we take your money.

I hate to see great designers waste their potential.

- Working a less-than-ideal 9 to 5
- With people you don’t love
- On a vision not yours

A shame.

If you can’t wait for the weekend
If you lack energy in the evening
If you struggle with consistency
If you hate starting on Mondays
If you rush home after 5 PM
If you fail to stay focused

You’re doing the wrong work.

Keep searching until you:

- Do work you love doing on your schedule
- With the people you love working with
- On a vision you truly align with

Aim for at least 80% of that.

100% is rarely achieved.

Most times:

→ This work is designed

Because I strongly believe:

Personalities don't fit a standardized job. 

You need to design a job unique to you.

And iteratively make this job a reality.

Just like you design new products.

Don't dread your 90.000 hours.

Start designing your work.

name: The Ethical Loophole
description: Addresses a controversial topic (like AI usage or 'cheating' algorithms) head-on. Owns the controversy to prove results.
original_post_text:
Is this cheating?

I trained AI on Alex Hormozi's writing style.

Now my posts get 1,000 likes.

People are calling it "unfair."

Good.

Here's what happened:

I was posting on LinkedIn for 6 months.

5-10 likes per post. Maybe.

Spending 2 hours per post.

Getting nowhere.

Then I realized something:

Why am I starting from scratch every time?

Alex Hormozi's posts get 10,000 likes.

Justin Welsh closes deals from every post.

Gary Vee built an empire here.

They found the formula.

So I asked: Can I train AI to copy it?

I fed AI their all 5000 posts.

Told it to learn:
• Hook patterns
• Sentence rhythm
• Story structure
• How they create tension

Then I told it: "Write about my business. Use their style."

First post: 587 likes
Third post: 843 likes  
15th post: 2,091 likes

Same topics I'd been writing about for months.

Different voice.

But I didn't stop there.

I analyzed 847,293 viral LinkedIn posts.

Found the patterns that make people engage.

Trained AI on all of them.

Now when I write about ANY topic:

AI finds every viral post about it.
Learns why they worked.
Copies those patterns for my content.

People are saying it's "cheating."

"You're not being authentic."
"That's not YOUR voice."
"Real creators don't use AI."

Here's my response:

Every creator studies other creators.
Every writer reads other writers.
Every musician learns from other musicians.

name: The Cosmetic Fix Takedown
description: Attacks surface-level tactics (like changing button colors) and provides a deep, foundational checklist for true success.
original_post_text:
I’ve lost count of how many landing page “fixes” I’ve seen that fix nothing.

Button color tests.
Block placements. 
Form lengths. 
Media types. 
CRO “hacks.”

A buyer isn't going to think a blue button suddenly makes this worth their time. 

Looking “optimized” isn’t the same as being useful.

And for B2B, THAT should be our aim. 

So when I’m auditing or building B2B landing pages, I’m looking for something different:

• Who (exactly) are we speaking to? 
• Are we using their language or ours?
• Are we talking about our product contextually?
• Are we setting clear expectations of the process?
• Can someone self-qualify without a sales call?
• Does the page deliver on the ad promise?
• Is there proof that actually supports the claim?
• Is pricing or comparison info accessible?
• Is the page easily consumable? 
• What is the ask:give ratio? 

If a B2B landing page isn’t helping someone make a decision, it’s just a pretty page with a scroll bar. 

All style, no substance.

name: The Academic Authority Hook

original_post_text:
MIT just exposed the hidden cost of using AI:
And it’s happening inside your brain right now:

AI doesn’t just change your workflow;
It rewires your mind.

Massachusetts Institute of Technology researchers tracked
 ↳ how people used ChatGPT to write essays.

They measured it with EEG scans to see what was really happening.

They tested three groups:

 ↳ People writing without help.
 ↳ People using Google Search.
 ↳ People using ChatGPT.

The results were insane.

Here’s what they found:

1/ Your brain stops trying:

 ↳ ChatGPT users had the lowest brain activity.
 ↳ Less focus, less effort, less memory.

2/ The more you used AI, the less your brain worked:

 ↳ Cognitive effort dropped with each task.
 ↳ Dependency grew quietly but quickly.

3/ You forget what you wrote:

 ↳ AI users couldn’t recall their own essays.
 ↳ They didn’t own their thinking.

4/ Once you switch to AI, it’s hard to go back:

 ↳ Even after returning to solo writing, performance stayed low.
 ↳ The brain had adapted to not thinking.

5/ Using AI first made everything worse:

 ↳ Those who started with ChatGPT had the weakest neural responses.
 ↳ But using search first, AI second preserved more brain function.

6/ What's the real cost?
 ↳ A creeping “cognitive debt.”
 ↳ You get more done now, but think less later

MIT’s research warns us:

AI boosts productivity but may dull your mind.

If you’re leading a team, building policy, or shaping the future of work:
 ↳ this is your wake-up call.

The tools we use shape how we think.

And right now, AI might be doing more thinking than we are.

What do you think about this?

Share your thoughts below.

♻️ Share with someone in your network who needs to see this

Your partner is figuring out their own mess.
And the world doesn’t stop for anyone.

You tweak the plan.
You journal about timing.
You call it strategy.

No one says it.
No one comes.

You send the message.
You take the leap.
Or you don’t.

Either way, it’s just you now.
It always was.

The sooner you stop looking for your parents in other people, the sooner you start acting like your own.

name: The Cautionary Tech Tale
description: Tells a story of a hyped, trendy tool (like AI coding) failing spectacularly in the real world. Positions the author as the voice of reason and reality.
original_post_text:
My friend vibe coded an app in 2 weeks. After 5 months, he’s still trying to fix bugs he couldn't understand.

When he started, he was so confident to go all-in with AI:

1, Cursor for backend logic
2, Loveable for UI components
3, Replit for deployments
4, Claude for code reviews

The early progress was promising.

Basic features worked well for himself.

UI components were clean.

Deployment setup was fast.

Until he let real users try the app, problems happened:

- Random crashes
- Memory leaks
- Database bottlenecks

After 5 months of struggling:

He already spent $5k on different AI tools.

When old bugs were fixed, new ones appeared.

Couldn't launch at all.

Finally, he had to hire an engineer to fix the mess.

And it even took the engineer a month to do it.

If you vibe code for personal use, it's perfect.

But risky for commercial purposes.

Without dev expertise, fixing is almost impossible.

Before vibe-coding:

Decide if it's for learning or a serious business.

Plan for real engineering support.

Don't compromise on security.

Everyone feels like a fraud at first.

The ones who make it just keep showing up anyway.

They post the awkward content.

They publish before they're ready.

And slowly, the embarrassment fades.

Because you realize that nobody's keeping score but you.

So if you've been holding back because you don't feel ready...

Start anyway!

And progress beats perfection every single time.

Ready to stop overthinking and start building?

I turned daily posts into 800K followers and $10M+ in revenue.

name: The Fake Strategy Call-Out
description: Calls out a specific department or role for confusing 'being busy' with 'being strategic.' It defines what real strategy looks like and challenges the reader to change.
original_post_text:
There's a billion-dollar lie in analytics.
"We're being strategic":

Most data teams think they're strategic.
But they're not.

They're often expensive support desks.

"We need this dashboard by Friday!"
"Can you pull these numbers real quick?"
"Why don't we have data on this yet?"

Sound familiar?

I see it everywhere:
Teams drowning in requests.
Analysts jumping from dashboard to dashboard.
Everyone busy, but nobody moving the needle.

Here's the brutal truth:

If everything is a priority, you don't have a strategy.

Most data teams have to-do lists, not strategies:

1/ They're responsive, not respected.

2/ They ship outputs, not outcomes.

3/ They're busy, but not strategic.

The result?

Your most talented analysts become human ticket machines.
Your insights get lost in the noise.
And your leadership questions your ROI.

But here's what strategy actually is:

A system for making decisions.

Not a fancy framework.
Not a quarterly planning session.
A system for choosing what matters.

Strategy helps you decide:
↳ Which problems are worth solving.
↳ Which requests are just noise. 
↳ What moves the business forward vs what feels urgent.

The best data teams get strategic by doing less.

But doing it deliberately.

They say no to good requests.
So they can say yes to great opportunities.

They build for impact, not activity.

Your data team isn't failing because of tools.
Your data team isn't failing because of talent.

It's failing because it lacks strategy.

How is your data team making strategic decisions?

What would change if you said no to half your current requests?

Share below. 

♻️ Share with a data leader who needs to see this.

name: The Stealth Launch Timeline
description: Documents the chaotic, multi-year journey of a product before dropping a massive success metric. Acts as a 'building in public' flex while launching a product.
original_post_text:
We just filled all 500 spots for Kleo 2.0 in 4 days.

But it's been a wild 2-year journey:

- Built a Chrome extension for myself
- Used it to study LinkedIn creators' posts
- Helped me get 100,000 followers in 1 year
- Gave it to a few friends to help them
- They loved it: “why not make it public?”
- Made it free on the Chrome store
- Grew it to over 70,000 users
- Started building the web version
- Received a C&D from LinkedIn
- Shut it down and pivoted the web app
- Assembled the A Team (Lara, Rob, Cameron)
- Rebuilt Kleo: The Cursor of LinkedIn posts
- Got feedback from a group of beta testers
- Opened another 500 discounted beta spots
- Filled all 500 spots in less than 4 days

I couldn't have predicted any of this.

From a personal tool to 70K users to a C&D to rebuilding even better, every setback taught me something.

Thank you to everyone who believed in the vision, especially during the darkest moments.

Kleo 2.0 is only just getting started.

PS. If you missed this round, join the waitlist in the comments. The next spots are opening soon.

Because people don’t connect with perfection.
They connect with truth.

and start relating,

name: The Failure vs Success Playbook
description: A stark dual-list format that breaks down exactly why people fail versus exactly what they should do to succeed. Highly scannable.
original_post_text:
Why most founders fail:

- Get excited about an idea
- Don’t talk to users because “I’d use it myself”
- Build for months without feedback
- Launch and… no one cares
- Add more features, hoping it’ll help
- Start burning cash on ads to get users
- Run out of money and time
- Shut it down
- Blame the bad timing, the market, or team

How successful founders actually operate:

- Start with a real problem
- Talk to 50+ people who experience that pain to validate it
- Build a super simple version for a specific user segment
- Make sure that small group loves it
- Charge money early, even if it feels scary
- Keep talking to users every week
- Hire slowly, stay lean, don’t overspend
- Only scale when the numbers work
- Grow gradually from 10 → 100 → 1,000 loyal users

That’s how it really works.
That’s how we’re building Fluently (AI English tutor → GetFluently.app).

original_post_text:
Your ICP isn’t ignoring you.
 
They’re avoiding you.
 
Because deep down, your content reminds
them of something they don’t want to face:
 
They have an actual problem.
 
But admitting it?
 
Means admitting they’ve failed somewhere.
Or that something isn’t working for them.
 
So they scroll past your “educational” posts.
They skip your “value-packed” carousels.
 
Not because it’s bad content.
 
Because it hits too close.
 
→ The quiet coach won’t like your sales post.
→ The stuck creator won’t like your growth post.
→ The broke founder won’t like your pricing post.
 
They’ll save it. Silently.
 
And come back weeks later when
the pain gets too loud to ignore.
 
So stop writing for validation.
Start writing to build trust.
 
When they’re finally ready to act,
you’ll be the first name they remember.
 
+ Stay loud.
+ Stay seen.
+ Stay patient.
 
Your content is working.
It’s just hitting where it hurts.

Ps: How was your weekend?

original_post_text:
i ran an experiment that would make most marketers uncomfortable.

we created a fake account on Twitter.

- hot girl profile pic
- tech industry focus
- started DMing founders

i doubt you’ll ever guess what happened…

0 to 4,000 followers in 2 weeks.

outbound became irrelevant because inbound flooded in.

attention is biased.

the same message from different accounts gets completely different responses.

i'm not saying this is right.

i'm saying this is reality.

so what actually matters for your marketing?

not your gender or looks.

but understanding what catches attention in your market.
→ Is it contrarian takes?
→ Is it visual content?
→ Is it who's saying it vs what's being said?

most people ignore the actual psychology at play.

they focus on "creating great content" without understanding the attention layer first.

test what gets noticed in your space.

then optimize from there.

the market doesn't care about fair.

it cares about what works.
